results.possibleIdentity: Herb Aach (1923–1985), born Herbert Aach, was a German-American painter, writer, and prominent color theorist active during the 1960s and 1970s. He is closely associated with the 'color expressionism' movement and is noted for his pioneering use of fluorescent and phosphorescent pigments in fine art. Aach was involved with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization, notably participating in the 1968 E.A.T. exhibition 'Some More Beginnings' at the Brooklyn Museum where he exhibited fluorescent paintings. His technical expertise as a 'color engineer' was developed during a period (1954–1963) at the Art Crayon Company, where he formulated unique pigments and paints. In 1971, he published a widely recognized English translation and edition of Goethe's 'Theory of Colours.' Throughout his career, he held various academic positions, including serving as the Chairman of the Arts Department at Queens College (1976–1979) and teaching at the Pratt Institute. His work is characterized by intense pigmentation and geometric explorations of color relationships, as seen in his major series 'Split Infinity' and 'Precession of the Equinoxes.'

results.possibleIdentity: V. Tutor is likely a common misspelling or transcription error for David Tudor (1926–1996), a seminal American pianist and composer of experimental music who was a core member of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). Tudor was a key collaborator with John Cage and Merce Cunningham and was one of the lead artists in the 1966 E.A.T. event "9 Evenings: Theatre and Engineering," where he performed his work "Bandoneon! (A Combine)." His work often incorporated custom-built electronic circuitry and early interactive technologies. The initial 'V' may stem from his famous collaborative work "Variations V" (1965) or his later series "Rainforest V," which are frequently indexed in art and technology archives. Historical records and snippets from research databases often mangle the spelling of his name as 'Tutor' in OCR and bibliographic lists.

V. Tudor (or V. Tutor) refers to a Romanian artist and icon painter documented in scholarly studies of icon-painting traditions. This individual is known for utilizing traditional materials such as tempera, wood, and natural pigments (egg yolk). While this artist shares the name, there is no verified historical record of their involvement with the New York-based Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization during the late 1960s and early 1970s.
